Description: Millennium Centre stands as the most frequented shopping destination in Aizawl, Mizoram. Visitors can discover contemporary fashion selections and modern dining establishments.

Situated in the heart of the city, this institution showcases Mizoram cultural legacy. The facility houses archaeological finds, historical photographs, and biological specimens. An entire section is devoted to Mizo tribal heritage.

A non-denominational church established by Dr. L.B. Sailo in 1984. Construction commenced in 1996 at Chawlhhmun in the Kidron Valley approximately 10 km from Aizawl city center. The 55m x 55m structure features four pillars with Davids stars and twelve main doors.
The best breathtaking view of Aizawl is from Hangi Lunglen Tlang. The adrenaline starts to kick in and the thrill starts from here.
A modern monument in Aizawl built as a memorial to a wife who died in a car accident in 2001. Located near Durtlang Hills with views of Aizawl.

Located less than an hours drive east of Aizawl this hilltop complex offers panoramic views of the city and surrounding lush green hills. Features a cafeteria luxurious cottages meditation centre and auditorium.

A recreated traditional Mizo village located in Falkawn approximately 18 km south of Aizawl. The site represents a historic Lusei village with the chiefs residence blacksmith workshop and a bachelors dormitory.
Hmuifang Tourist Resort is situated at the scenic peak of Hmuifang mountain approximately 50 km south of Aizawl at an elevation of 1619 meters. The mountain remains covered with virgin forests.
One of the best breathtaking views of Aizawl City is from Thuampui and Falkland sides where one can see half of Aizawl City.
Colonial structure erected in 1890 serving as the Deputy Commissioners Office for Aizawl District.
A heritage site for Assam Rifles built by Lt Col GH Loch in the late 1890s. Features 18th century cannons facing the city of Aizawl.
Built by Zosap Missionary Pu Zosaphluia in 1898. Originally a Mission School. Recently handed over to Mizoram Synod after 68 years.
A small waterfall in Aizawl one of the natural treasures the region offers. Its scenic beauty attracts visitors seeking a weekend getaway.
Located in Lungverh on the outskirts of Aizawl City maintained by the State Forest Department.
A recreational entertainment facility designed as a safe open space for friends and family.
A historic religious structure maintained by Mizoram Synod in Mission Veng Aizawl. Built by missionaries during the 1890s now serves as the Synod Moderator residence.
A century-old residence of Mizo Chief Khamliana Sailo at Lungleng village. Built in 1918 it was the first house in Mizoram with a tin roof. Located 30 km from Aizawl.
A nature-focused recreational destination offering canopy walks and forest experiences. Located 15 minutes from Aizawl City with birdwatching opportunities.
One of the best parks in Aizawl situated in Muthi village approximately 15 minutes drive from the city. Excellent for family outings.
A trekking destination offering early morning hikes with opportunities to witness sunrise and morning mists.
A bird-watching hotspot nestled in virgin forest approximately 76 km from Aizawl with diverse avian species.
An artificial lake at North Tinghmun in Aizawl District. Offers boating and accommodation managed by the Ting Dil Cooperative Society.
Rolling hills extending across hundreds of miles featuring lush green forests bamboo groves paddy fields wildlife dramatic cliffs and serene lakes.
A spectacular mountain destination with breathtaking views from the summit. Known as a hiking destination suitable for outdoor enthusiasts.
A one-day camping experience across four Aizawl district villages. Includes nature walks through unexplored forests tree houses hanging bridges and sunset views.
A waterfall approximately 11.1 km from Aizawl city featuring natural cascading water formations.
A breathtaking waterfall near Phulpui that captivates visitors with its exceptional natural display.
A memorial stone at Tachhip village 20 km from Aizawl. Erected by a Palian chief approximately 300 years ago.
An inspection bungalow constructed during the British colonial period in the then Lushai Hills now Mizoram.
A scenic waterfall at Tuirial River in Muallungthu approximately 35 km from Aizawl.
A waterfall in Hualngohmun village approximately 25 km from Aizawl at 980 meters altitude.
A scenic natural destination 45 minutes from Aizawl ideal for escaping urban life. Includes visits to Reiek Tlang hilltop.
An aquatic entertainment venue in Aizawl featuring water rides rejuvenating pools and a kids zone set against scenic landscapes.

Tuirum Li over Nghasih Lui is an adventure destination showcasing nature's beauty. Described as 'one of the rarest travel experiences, toughest and the dearest,' it offers intimate encounters with natural landscapes and represents a challenging journey worth experiencing.
The place where first Missionaries Rev JH Lorrain (Pu Buanga) and Rev FW Savidge (Sap Upa) arrived in Mizoram. A memorial stone was created by TKP, Youth Association of Baptist Church of Mizoram.

Tlabung Post Office is the first Post Office in Mizoram, established 150 years ago. Located in a bustling town near the Indo-Bangladesh border, it serves as the main trade route between the two countries at the local level. Accessible from Aizawl via Lunglei within 9 hours by car. Accommodation available at Tourist Lodge, Forest IB, PWD IB, and private lodges.

Tlabung Police Station was constructed by the British and established in 1889. Located at the South-Western edge of Lunglei District close to the Indo-Bangladesh Border, the station sits atop a hill accessible by an uphill road from Tlabung town center. It is recognized as the oldest Police Station Building in Mizoram.

Tlabung Hospital was established in 1872 and is considered the oldest hospital in Mizoram. Located 250.1 km from Aizawl with an altitude of 61 meters, it sits approximately 1.4 km from the nearest tourist lodge and is about 20 minutes away on foot.

A 145-year-old heritage building established in 1871 by Tom Herbert Lewin's men, Tlabung Bungalow served as Mizoram's first Inspection Bungalow. Constructed using wood, bamboo, zinc, plate-glass, and stone, it remains a sought-after tourist lodge. Despite being handed to the Public Work Department, it continues attracting visitors who prefer this historic structure over newer cottages. The Young Mizo Association recently installed a granite marker commemorating its establishment and other heritage buildings.
Blessed with natural beauty and unlimited adventure, Mizoram never ceases to amaze with geological marvels such as Lungding in Rualalung, Lunglei District.

Stone figures in meditational Buddhist poses located on a rocky portion of Tawikhawthlir hill near South Mualcheng village, approximately 65 km south of Lunglei. It is not known as to who created them and remains a mystery till date since no other relics of Buddhism is found elsewhere in the state.

Khawnglung is situated approximately 160 km from Aizawl. The sanctuary features steep cliff formations that have historically provided natural shelter for humans and wildlife. It hosts diverse flora and fauna including wild boar, gibbon, barking deer, sambar deer, hoolock, and leopard. Optimal visitation period spans October through March, requiring coordination with the Department of Environment and Forest, Mizoram. Adjacent villages include Pangzawl, Rawpui, Bungtlang, Sialsir, Lungchhuan, and Chawngtui.

Darkhuang Tlang, or 'Gong Mountain,' is located in Pukpui village about 7 km from Lunglei. This site is historically significant as the place where Darphawka, a 19th-century Mizo prophet, claimed to communicate with spirits and received visions of future events, including the arrival of Christian missionaries. A prayer house has been constructed on the peak for visitors. Recently, skeletal remains were recovered and collected for forensic analysis and carbon dating.

A cemetery of early Christians situated at Theiriat village near Lunglei. Before pioneer missionaries arrived, Sehan Roy evangelised Mizo labourers, converting 40 families by 1900. These harassed converts established a Christian village in Theiriat. Their gravestones bear Bible verse quotations and inscriptions in English, representing early Christian heritage in Mizoram.

Phunhnawma Lung Lungpuitlang, literally 'Phunhnawma's Rock', is located in Lungpuitlang village, 106 km east of Lunglei near the Myanmar border. This large rock measures approximately 60 metres with a height of roughly 26.60 metres. Situated atop a breezy hill at 5058 ft altitude, it offers panoramic views of surrounding hill ranges including western Myanmar. According to local legend, the rock was discovered during a hunting trip by Phunhnawma, chief of Cheural village. Upon arrival, fairy guardians instructed him to return home, bring a cock as an offering, and in exchange they would bless him with abundant wild animals to hunt. He complied, and the fairies fulfilled their promise, establishing his reputation as a great hunter.
A sprawling mountain near Lunglei with rich diversity in nature and culture that attracts many tourists. One of the most fascinating sites in the District.

Kau Tlabung Falls is a tourist attraction located in Thenhlum, Serchhip District, Mizoram. The page provides basic information about the location with an altitude of 100 meters, situated approximately 165.4 km from Aizawl and 109.4 km from the nearest tourist lodge. The site requires approximately 1560 minutes of walking distance to access.

A century-old villa constructed in 1914 by Christian Missionaries, located in Serkawr between Tuipang and Kaladan, 288 km from Aizawl. The site represents a pioneering Baptist missionary settlement where J Herbert Lorraine and F.W Savidge first arrived in 1907 to preach the gospel. The property features preserved century-old furniture, furnishings, and decorative items. The current resident, Mrs. Violet Lorrain Foxall (granddaughter of J Herbert Lorrain), lives here with her family and welcomes visitors.

Palak Dil or Palak Lake is the largest natural lake in Mizoram situated near Phura in the southern part of the state. Oval in shape, it covers about 1 sq.km and is surrounded by lush virgin forests rich in flora and fauna. The lake features a rich legend about its origin involving a village, a serpent, and magical flooding. Nature enthusiasts are drawn to the area for its wildlife variety, including wetland birds, hill birds, migrating Pintail Ducks, and elephants in surrounding forests. A jeepable road connects Palak Lake from Phura while efforts maintain its pristine environmental state.

The Tokalo Wildlife Sanctuary is the second largest wildlife reserve in Mizoram, located in Siaha district covering approximately 250 sq. km. Declared a sanctuary in 2007 and managed by the Environment and Forest Department of the Mara Autonomous District Council, it features Moist Deciduous and Semi-evergreen Forest ecosystems. The sanctuary hosts diverse mammals, birds, amphibians, reptiles, and is particularly noted for its butterfly variety. Accessible from Phura via private vehicles and local buses, the optimal visiting period runs from October through April.

The Castle of Beino (also called Bawinu) is described as 'a lesser-explored grand canyon of Northeast India' formed by the Kolodyne/Kaladan river. The geological formation features rock structures resembling castle quarters, including sections that look like soldiers at the entrance, a kitchen, and a great hall. The crystalline river water creates reflections of the formations. Best visited February to May; motorboats and local guides available. The area supports wildlife and bird watching. Access via Saphaw village (boat journey from Lomasu), reached through Lungdar or Taidang.

Mount Mawma Ecological Park is located in Chakhei Village of Siaha District, standing at 6,725 feet. It is the third highest peak in Northeast India and offers a unique vantage point where three countries are visible on clear winter days: Myanmar, Mizoram, and Bangladesh. The park features brick trekking paths, multiple rest points, a suspension bridge, and a watch tower. Seasonal flowering trees including rhododendron and cherry blossoms enhance the natural beauty.

A rocky mound with flat top and sheer sides located near Vaphai village close to the Myanmar border. Legend tells of Thasiama, a man of extraordinarily long life, whose pregnant mithun (cow) mysteriously appeared atop this rocky formation. The name translates to 'a place where Thasiama's mithun gave birth to a calf.' Local accounts mention traces of an ancient village and overgrown pathways leading toward Myanmar in the surrounding dense vegetation.

A rocky ledge situated at Dungtlang village, about 56 kilometers from Champhai, commanding views of surrounding hills. Named after Lianchhiari, daughter of chief Vanhnuaithanga, who sat here watching the distant village where her lover Chawngfianga had migrated after their planned marriage was thwarted by a jealous go-between. She spent days composing sorrowful love songs while gazing toward Chhingzawl village.

Champhai is a fascinating hill town on the eastern side of Mizoram, magical year-round with enhanced charm during winter when temperatures fall below freezing. The destination features rolling hills and beautiful valleys, making it worth visiting.

MURA PUK (Mura Caves) located at the outskirts of Zote village near Champhai, Mizoram. According to legend, these caves served as refuge from a monstrous eagle called 'Mura'. Originally seven caves existed when Zote village was resettled in 1899; only four survive today. The mythical creature was described as ferocious with talons as large as female mithun horns. People fled to these stone-doored caves when Mura attacked settlements.
Mangkhai Lung is located in Zotlang East, Champhai. A monolith site with minimal descriptive information available.

Murlen National Park, situated near the Indo-Myanmar border in Champhai District approximately 245 km from Aizawl, is recognized as one of the few remaining sanctuaries for the state bird, Vavu (Mrs Hume's Pheasant). The park encompasses 100 square kilometers of pristine forests, featuring subtropical evergreen and sub-montane forest ecosystems that harbor diverse mammals and bird species. Notable geological features include sheer rocky cliffs with honeycomb formations inhabited by swifts and swallows. The park is particularly suited for bird-watching activities, with optimal visiting conditions from October through May. Access requires coordination with the Department of Environment and Forest.

Located near Farkawn village on a rockface, this cave contains human skulls and bones, estimated to be about three hundred years old, along with pieces of traditional shawls. While the precise identities and reasons for their placement remain unexplained, it is commonly assumed they were left by people of the erstwhile Lamsial village when Mizos settled in this area after crossing the Tiau river bordering Myanmar.

Kungawrhi Puk is one of the most significant and largest caves in Mizoram, located on hills between Farkawn and Vaphai villages in Champhai district. According to legend, spirits once confined a beautiful girl named Kungawrhi in these caves. Her betrothed, Pnathira, bravely fought the spirits and freed her. The cave was named after this girl. Visitors can reach Kungawrhi Puk by hiring a tourist vehicle from Champhai town.

A legendary peak featuring interesting Mizo folk tales and songs, situated at the junction of three villages: Vaphai, Chawngtui, and Farkawn. Elevation is 6,356 feet above sea level, near Vaphai village.

Samthang Zopui Phul is a tourist destination developed under NEC funding by the Deputy Commissioner of Champhai District. It offers a perfect setting for day excursion and weekend getaway with friends and families.

Mizo Hlakungpui Mual is a monument commemorating great Mizo poets, established in 1986 at Khawbung village's southern edge. The site features monuments erected at 10-year intervals honoring laureate poets and writers. It includes an orchid park and museum preserving the laureates' belongings. The initiative began in 1983 when Khawbung's YMA Executive Committee resolved to commemorate two poets. Aizawl is approximately 265 kilometers away.

In the vicinity of Vangchhia, close to the Myanmar border, about 50 km south of Champhai, there are a large number of menhir stones erected in lines called Kawtchhuah Ropui or 'the great entranceway'. These commemoration stones, numbering 171 in total, feature embossed carvings of human beings and animals. The village was named after the Vangchhia clan, the earliest settlers, and these stones are attributed to them.

The lost city of Vangchhia is the largest necropolis site in the world, located in Champhai District near Khawbung Village. It features 171 ancient carved menhir stones that became Mizoram's first protected archaeological site in 2012. The site includes ancient graves and a 200-meter-long water pavilion, offering insights into a lost civilization. Distance from Aizawl is 250 kilometers.

Located in Lungphunlian village in Champhai District, about 45 km north of Champhai town, this site features a large monolith measuring 13.5 ft in height, roughly 6.5 ft in breadth, and 2 ft in thickness -- possibly the largest of its kind in Mizoram. Without any engravings or carvings, its origins remain unclear, though it's generally believed to have been erected by nomadic Meitei people, supported by inscriptions on a nearby smaller stone. The site is accessible by local taxis and buses, attracting archaeological enthusiasts and tourists throughout the year.
Lungphun ropui at Lianpui village in Champhai District, Mizoram is a cluster of megaliths in the eastern part of Mizoram.

The picturesque Serlui lake is located about an hour drive from Kolasib Town and is a perfect weekend destination. Formed with the construction of the dam of the Serlui Hydel Project, the riverine lake extends upriver and meanders through the valley of River Serlui, spreading out into wide pools at many points in the midst of thick forests and coursing between lush-green hills. The 53 km long inundation of the valley can be seen even from the NH-54. The Department of Tourism has developed an accommodation infrastructure which is expected to be commissioned shortly. Plans are also being drawn for development of water sport facilities and Eco-Tourism center. The Department of Tourism has also developed a Pisciculture center using the dam as its base.

Located in Kolasib, Bak Puk (also known as Tui Lut) is a cave system featuring a vertical narrow entrance that functions like a sinkhole, collecting rainwater. The tunnel expands into wide caverns with striking rock formations and deep pools that remain largely unexplored. The cave system has no identified outlet, spawning local legends about an underground lake beneath the town. An expedition in January 2016 by the 'Zotrek' group explored the cave to approximately 100 meters depth, revealing exquisite rock structures and multiple chambers.

Pualreng Wildlife Sanctuary features fringe villages including N. Hlimen, Thingthelh, Bukpui, Palsang, Zohmun, and N. Khawdungsei. The sanctuary hosts diverse bird species such as great Indian hornbill, wreathed hornbill, pied hornbill, Khaleeej pheasant, Bhutan peacock pheasant, and red jungle fowl. Wildlife includes arboreal animals like Hoolock gibbon, slow lorises, and common langurs, plus terrestrial species such as Himalayan black bears, leopards, sambars, barking deer, binturongs, and Chinese pangolins.
Lung nupa in Bualpui at Kolasib District! Be an explorer of unspoiled destination and step outside your comfort zone for unlimited adventure to remote areas and possibly hostile destinations.

Rated as one of the best all season Golf courses in the North East India, Thenzawl Golf Course & Resort offers Pay & Play facility for all ability levels. It is the first and only golf course funded by the Ministry of Tourism, Government of India. Designed by Graham Cooke and Associates, a top-ranked Canada based Golf Course architectural firm. The 105-acre site features a 75-acre 18-hole golf course with shared fairways and automated sprinkler irrigation. Facilities include 30 Eco-Log Huts, Cafeteria, Open Air Food Court, Reception area and Waiting Lounge, constructed with Siberian Pinewood and world-class furniture. Located in a peaceful setting with breathtaking views, ideal for beginners.
Located in the outskirt of Thenzawl with eco log huts, spa and massage centre, after a hectic trip at Thenzawl for pampering yourself during the weekend.

The Vantawng Falls is the highest waterfall in Mizoram state, ranked 13th among India's highest waterfalls. This two-tiered waterfall descends from 229 meters (750 feet) in the Vanva river near Thenzawl. Named after Vantawnga, a legendary swimmer, the falls are situated 30 km from Serchhip and 97.8 km from Aizawl. A viewing tower provides comfortable access despite challenging forested terrain surrounding the location.

Tuirihiau Falls is the most spectacular waterfall in Mizoram's fast-flowing rivers, located in Vanva river near Buangpui. Its unique feature includes caves behind the falls, allowing views from behind the cascade.
Dilpui is a destination managed and maintained by the AH & Veterinary Department. Located 94 km from Aizawl at an altitude of 745 meters, it is situated 5.1 km from the nearest tourist lodge.

Vety Farm is managed and maintained by the AH & Veterinary Department. Located at an altitude of 745 meters, it is situated 94 kilometers from Aizawl with the nearest tourist lodge just 5.1 kilometers away.

Chawngchilhi Puk is a mesmerizing cave with an enthralling folktale. According to legend, a young woman fell in love with a serpent and met him in secret. When her father discovered this, he killed the serpent and his daughter. The Mizo people have preserved this authentic tale, with their roots enchained in stories reflecting the uniqueness of their culture.
Located in Thenzawl, this is the tomb of Mizo Chief Bengkhuaia Sailo who captured an English girl named Mary Winchester.

Thenzawl Deer Park is an interesting tourist destination with most famous places located nearby such as Vantawng, Tuirihiau, Golf, Wellness, Vaibiak, Tualvungi Thlan, Bengkhuaia Thlan, Chawngchilhi Puk, Lau River & Falls.

Lungkhawdur translates as 'Cloudy Stone' and features a large flat stone with elliptical shapes resembling alto cumulus cloud formations. Located near Vanchengpui village in Serchhip district, its origin -- whether natural or man-made -- remains uncertain. Deep grooves at upper and lower ends suggest possible sculptural work, attracting tourists with its intriguing and mysterious rock mass.

The Castle of Bawinu/Beino is a lesser-explored geological formation in Mizoram, often called the 'Grand Canyon of Mizoram.' Located along the Kolodyne/Kaladan River, it features spectacular rock structures resembling castle quarters. The formation is accessible February to May; motorboat and local guides are recommended. The crystalline river creates beautiful reflections of the structures, and the area is home to diverse wildlife and birds.

The cave is located on the southeastern part of Phawngpui mountain. According to legend, Tialpari was a girl in love with warrior Dopawrha. When her parents arranged marriage to a distant chief, she fled to the cave. Villagers found her but provided food and waited for her lover, who eventually came and married her.

The highest peak in Mizoram at 2,157 metres, Phawngpui (Blue Mountain) sits near the state's south-eastern border. This 50 sq.km national park features a 10 km ridge with sheer cliffs, including the spectacular Thlazuang Kham. The landscape showcases flowering rhododendrons, orchids, and grassy glades ideal for camping. Wildlife includes gorals, sunbirds, peregrine falcons, barking deer, and hoolock gibbons. Notable bird species are Blyth's Tragopan, Dark Rumped Swift, and Mrs. Hume's Pheasant (state bird). Endangered mammals include slow lorises, tigers, leopards, serows, Asian black bears, and langurs.

The sanctuary comprises deciduous and tropical green forests. It is home to elephants, gaurs and other mammals and a great variety of primates. It is also rich in birdlife including peacock pheasant, pigeons and hornbills.

Paithar Tlang is situated in Paithar village within Lawngtlai District, approximately 15km from Lawngtlai town. The location features an altitude of 1302 meters and is positioned 263km from Aizawl, with the nearest tourist lodge approximately 20km away. A 60-minute walking distance provides access to this natural destination.

In Ailawng near Reiek, approximately 30 km west of Aizawl, lies a cave named after Khuangchera, a legendary Mizo warrior from the late nineteenth century. According to local legend, villagers obtained water from a spring flowing from a narrow cave entrance that most feared to enter due to superstition. Debate persisted about whether another cave on the opposite hillside connected to it, as water flowed in without visible outlet elsewhere. Khuangchera courageously explored using a large bamboo torch and emerged from the opposite cave entrance, proving the two mouths connected through an underground passage. He subsequently fell seriously ill, intensifying villagers' fear. Full exploration occurred only in the early 1980s by a local adventure club.

Reiek Hills is a trekking destination offering natural treasures, caves, rivers, and waterfalls. Located 25.3 km from Aizawl at an altitude of 1208m, the site features adventure activities including cycling and hiking. The nearest tourist lodge is 1.2 km away.

Dampa Tiger Reserve is a 500 sq km tiger reserve in western Mizoram's Lushai Hills at 800-1,100m altitude, declared in 1994 as part of Project Tiger. The tropical forests feature diverse flora and fauna with steep hills, valleys, jungle streams, and natural salt licks. Forest exploration requires walking rather than vehicle access. A tiger was recently spotted after a seven-year absence.

Thawmpawnga Cave in Ailawng, located just one hour drive from Aizawl. The area offers adventure activities and sightseeing with natural beauty and breathtaking views amidst verdant forest. This cave represents one of many must-visit destinations to experience the breathtaking sight.

Leisang Kurung at Ailawng offers diverse enjoyment opportunities with breathtaking panoramas and incredible sights. This natural wonder features rocky walls and grandiose floors, inviting travelers to immerse themselves in the mystery of this unique geological formation.
Tuisen Falls is a stunning waterfall located in Reiek village, described as 'a jaw-dropping beauty and awe-inspiring waterfalls' situated off the beaten path.
Located in Reiek, Mamit District, Tuisen Tlabung Falls is described as 'a unique God gifted natural treasure with hidden beauty.' Accommodation is available at the nearby Reiek Tourist Resort.

Reng Dil is a man-made lake in Mamit District, approximately 150 km northwest of Aizawl and about 8 km from Zamuang village. Unlike other natural lakes in the region, this artificial lake offers opportunities for nature enthusiasts and birdwatchers.

Pukzing Puk, literally meaning 'A Cave of Shouting Stone', is located near Pukzing village northwest of Aizawl in Mamit district. The spacious cave features three chambers of approximately 4 square meters each connected by narrow passages. Local folklore suggests villagers historically heard evil spirits shouting within the cave, and legend claims the character Maurawkela hid a magical drum inside. The village has about 113 houses and 537 residents. Pukzing is recognized as one of Mizoram's largest caves and a must-visit destination for explorers.

Located in Rengdil village in Mamit District, Reng Puk is a destination featuring caving and heritage attractions. It sits at 48 meters altitude, 127 kilometers from Aizawl, with the nearest tourist lodge 45 kilometers away and approximately 3 minutes walking distance to reach the site.

The village derives its name from the flower 'Chawngtlai', which is abundant in nature. Inhabitants are developing it into a flower village. It features historical sites, artifacts, and monuments. Lungsen, a distinctive red-colored rock, is believed to tell fortunes.

Tea Garden in Biate - the cleanest town in Mizoram, a compact hill station on the eastern side of the state. Famous for its tea gardens and exceptional cleanliness standards practiced long before the Swachh Bharat Abhiyan. The community maintains remarkably clean streets with virtually no garbage visible, representing a model village envisioned by Mahatma Gandhi.
Keivawm Puk is a least visited and least known cave in Mizoram located in Ngaizawl village, Khawzawl District. Its inherent natural landscape features have attracted many to explore this destination.
Located in Lamzawl village, Khawzawl District, approximately 9km southeast of Ngopa village and 2.5km south of Lengteng peak. This cave destination offers a natural caving experience.

Tam Dil, meaning 'lake of mustard,' is located near Saitual village, approximately 110 km from Aizawl. It has been developed as a pisciculture centre and holiday resort. The lake and surrounding forest host diverse tree, plant, and animal species. According to local folklore, a widow's mustard plant was destroyed by her remarried husband, causing water to spring from the ground and fill the valley, creating the lake.

Tawi Wildlife Sanctuary is situated about 101 km from Aizawl, covering approximately 35.75 sq. km at altitudes between 400m-1300m. Established in 1978, it features tropical evergreen and semi-evergreen forests housing rare and endangered species. Flora includes canes, bamboos, Michelia champaca, and other species. Fauna encompasses tigers, leopards, sambars, barking deer, hoolock gibbons, and various bird species. Basic accommodation available at Hualtu Range H.Q.

Lengteng Wildlife Sanctuary is a protected area in Saitual district in eastern Mizoram, northeast India. It is an alpine forest containing the second highest peak in Mizoram and is specially noted for conservation of rare bird species. Declared a protected area in 1999 and a national wildlife sanctuary on 31 May 2001. Notable birds include dark-rumped swift, grey sibia, Mrs. Hume's pheasant, grey peacock pheasant, Oriental pied hornbill, rufous-bellied eagle, and white-naped yuhina. Blyth's tragopan was reported in a 2011 survey. Animal species include tiger, leopard, sambar deer, barking deer, goral, serow, hoolock gibbon, rhesus macaque, wild boars, and the vulnerable northern pig-tailed macaque.

Rungdil Lake is a magnificent twin lake situated 14 km from Suangpuilawn village. It covers an area of 2.5 hectares and is filled with the magic of Nature. Once home to large numbers of partridges, it remains popular with bird watchers and nature lovers. The lake consists of two similar bodies of water separated by a narrow strip of land, believed to have a subterranean connection.

Tawi Puk is a cave located in Hmuntha that represents one of Mizoram's magnificent offbeat destinations. The page notes it as a 'bucket list worthy' attraction for those seeking new travel experiences in the region.

Teikhang Kurung is located near Teikhang Village in Saitual District, approximately 8 hours' drive from Aizawl City. The site features magnificent wonder of nature and its rocky walls with spectacular geological formations that connect visitors with the natural world. The location stands at an altitude of 1,531 meters, roughly 223 kilometers from Aizawl, with the nearest tourist lodge 43 kilometers away and a 24-minute walking distance to reach it.

At Suangpuilawn village in Aizawl district there is a stone with inscriptions which have not been deciphered as yet. The stone slab of the size of 1m length and 1m width is placed by the side of the stream.

Hriangmual is a majestic peak in eastern Mizoram's Saitual District, approximately 180 km from Aizawl. Named after an evergreen 'Hriang' tree, it stands near Phuaibuang Village at 1,812 meters altitude. The largely unexplored destination features evergreen forests hosting wildlife including stag, deer, wild boar, and monkeys. Talan River flows through the forest, and Fangfar Puk cave contains stalagmites with water believed to have healing properties. Monolithic remains from the Thado and Ngendal tribes (late 1800s) dot the area, including six preserved stone tables. INTACH Mizoram Chapter conducts ongoing historical research. Optimal visiting period: November-March.

Pitch tents out in the wild and party your way at Darzo Tlang, Hnahthial District that will let you unwind and indulge in the goodness of being amidst nature and partying under the star studded skies far away from cityscapes. Located in Darzo in Hnahthial District. Tourists can also experience community fishing and angling in the village.
A serene village in Hnahthial District that offers family picnic on weekend getaway.
